narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.20—right around the typical 3.21 average for students in this dataset—your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try. The model estimated a 41.6% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which crossed the threshold for rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting students predicted to pass with high confidence. This decision hinged on a few key aspects of your profile, compared against patterns from thousands of past students' academic records, family backgrounds, and test scores. The most influential factor pulling in a positive direction—toward predicting you'd pass—was your undergraduate GPA; even though it's average, it stood out as a stabilizing strength in your case, suggesting your college performance signals better bar readiness than the model might expect otherwise. Coming in second for positive influence was your family's middle income bracket, which aligns with typical levels and seems to have reassured the model about your support system for law school success. However, the third most important factor worked against you: your Year 3 school decile ranking of 3, well below the average of about 5.5, which pushed the prediction toward failure by highlighting relatively weaker performance in that later stage compared to peers. Your LSAT score of 30, notably lower than the 36.7 average, and Year 1 decile of 3 also played roles, though less decisively, in tipping the overall balance. As a full-time student like over 90% of applicants, that wasn't a differentiator here. In essence, while your GPA and family background kept the failure odds from being higher, the dip in your later-year school rankings stood out as the key concern relative to typical students who pass the bar. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male applicant—many in similar demographics succeed with stronger decile trends—but a data-driven flag on bar passage risk. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it above average, strengthening your later-year grades if possible through additional coursework, or targeting schools with more holistic reviews that weigh your full profile. You could also seek bar prep resources early to build a track record countering this prediction, and reapply once you've addressed these gaps. With targeted improvements, your GPA foundation positions you well for a stronger outcome next time.
